examples.title: Fetch-Decode-Execute Cycle
examples.description: Demonstrates the basic instruction cycle performed by CPU
examples.code:
ALGORITHM FetchDecodeExecute
BEGIN
  WHILE programRunning = TRUE DO
    // FETCH PHASE
    address = PC                      // Get address of next instruction
    MAR = address                     // Memory Address Register
    instruction = READ(MAR)           // Fetch instruction from memory
    IR = instruction                  // Store in Instruction Register
    PC = PC + 1                       // Increment Program Counter
    
    // DECODE PHASE
    opcode = DECODE(IR)               // Extract operation code
    operands = EXTRACT(IR)            // Extract operand addresses
    
    // EXECUTE PHASE
    SETCONTROL(opcode)                // Set control signals for ALU
    RESULT = EXECUTE(opcode, operands) // Perform the operation
    
    // STORE PHASE
    IF RESULT_requires_store THEN
      STORE(RESULT, address)
    END IF
  END WHILE
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the four phases of instruction processing. The FETCH phase retrieves the instruction from memory using the address in PC. The DECODE phase interprets what operation needs to be performed. The EXECUTE phase performs the actual computation using ALU. The STORE phase writes the result back to memory if needed.

examples.title: Simple CPU Register Simulation
examples.description: Demonstrates basic CPU register operations and instruction processing
examples.code:
class SimpleCPU:
    def __init__(self):
        self.PC = 0          # Program Counter
        self.IR = 0          # Instruction Register
        self.ACC = 0         # Accumulator
        self.MAR = 0         # Memory Address Register
        self.MBR = 0         # Memory Buffer Register
        self.memory = [0] * 256  # Simple 256-byte memory
        
    def fetch(self, address):
        self.MAR = address
        self.IR = self.memory[self.MAR]  # Fetch instruction
        self.PC += 1
        
    def execute(self):
        opcode = self.IR >> 8        # Upper 8 bits = opcode
        operand = self.IR & 0xFF     # Lower 8 bits = operand
        
        if opcode == 1:              # LOAD
            self.ACC = self.memory[operand]
        elif opcode == 2:            # STORE
            self.memory[operand] = self.ACC
        elif opcode == 3:            # ADD
            self.ACC += self.memory[operand]
        elif opcode == 4:            # SUB
            self.ACC -= self.memory[operand]
        elif opcode == 5:            # JUMP
            self.PC = operand
        elif opcode == 0:            # HALT
            return False
        return True
    
    def run_program(self, program):
        for i, instr in enumerate(program):
            self.memory[i] = instr
        self.PC = 0
        running = True
        while running:
            self.fetch(self.PC)
            running = self.execute()

# Example: Load value at address 10, add value at 11, store result at 12
cpu = SimpleCPU()
cpu.memory[10] = 5    # First number
cpu.memory[11] = 3    # Second number
program = [0x010A, 0x030B, 0x020C, 0x0000]  # LOAD, ADD, STORE, HALT
cpu.run_program(program)
print(f"Result: {cpu.memory[12]}")  # Output: 8
examples.explanation: This Python code simulates a simple CPU with essential registers. The fetch() method retrieves instructions from memory, and execute() method performs operations based on opcode. This demonstrates how actual CPUs process instructions step by step.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(m) where m is memory size

examples.title: DMA Transfer Simulation
examples.description: Demonstrates how DMA allows direct memory access without CPU intervention
examples.explanation: This C code simulates DMA (Direct Memory Access) operation. Notice how the DMA transfer function allows the data transfer to happen independently. In real systems, the CPU can perform other computations while DMA handles the I/O transfer, significantly improving overall system throughput.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) transfer time but CPU time is O(1)

examples.title: Interrupt Handler Simulation
examples.description: Demonstrates interrupt-based I/O handling vs polling
examples.explanation: This Java code demonstrates the difference between interrupt-driven I/O and polling. In interrupt mode, the CPU can perform useful work and only stops to handle I/O when needed. In polling mode, the CPU wastes cycles continuously checking device status. This illustrates why interrupts are preferred for efficient system design.
examples.timeComplexity: Interrupt: O(n) useful work + O(k) interrupts; Polling: O(n*k) wasted checks
